title: Foundations of Democracy — DBQ Assessment
subject: Social Studies
essential_question: Is civic participation a right, a duty, or both? Use evidence from the documents and your knowledge of democratic principles to support your argument.
instructions: Read each document carefully. Answer the scaffolding questions for each document. Then write a well-organized essay responding to the essential question. Your essay must reference at least 4 of the 5 documents and include outside information from our unit.

documents.title: Pericles' Funeral Oration (431 BCE, adapted)
documents.source: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War
documents.excerpt: We do not say that a man who takes no interest in politics minds his own business; we say that he has no business here at all. We Athenians decide public questions for ourselves — we regard the citizen who holds aloof from public affairs not as harmless, but as useless.
documents.scaffolding_question: According to Pericles, what is the duty of an Athenian citizen? How does Athens view citizens who do not participate?

documents.title: John Locke, Second Treatise of Government (1689, adapted)
documents.excerpt: The people shall be judge; for who shall be judge whether his trustee or deputy acts well and according to the trust reposed in him, but he who deputes him?
documents.scaffolding_question: What role does Locke believe the people should play in government? How does this connect to the idea of 'consent of the governed'?

documents.title: U.S. Voter Turnout Data, 1960-2024
documents.excerpt: Presidential election voter turnout: 1960: 63.1%, 1980: 52.6%, 2000: 54.2%, 2016: 59.2%, 2020: 66.8%, 2024: 65.5%. Midterm election turnout has averaged 40% since 1970.
documents.scaffolding_question: What trend do you notice in voter turnout? What might explain why midterm turnout is consistently lower than presidential election turnout?

documents.title: Letter from a High School Student to the Editor (2024)
documents.excerpt: People say voting is our most important right, but what about volunteering, attending town halls, or just staying informed? I can't vote yet, but I organized a food drive that fed 200 families. Isn't that civic participation too?
documents.scaffolding_question: What forms of civic participation does the student describe beyond voting? Do you agree that these count as meaningful civic engagement?

documents.title: Australian Compulsory Voting Law (1924, adapted summary)
documents.excerpt: In Australia, all eligible citizens are required by law to vote in federal, state, and local elections. Failure to vote without a valid excuse results in a fine of $20-$50 AUD. Since compulsory voting began, turnout has consistently exceeded 90%.
documents.scaffolding_question: What is the argument for making voting mandatory? What might be an argument against it?

thesis.description: Clear, arguable thesis that directly addresses the essential question

document_analysis.description: References at least 4 documents with accurate analysis (not just summary)

outside_information.description: Includes relevant outside information from class discussions and readings

organization.description: Logical structure with introduction, body paragraphs, and conclusion

writing_quality.description: Clear writing with appropriate academic vocabulary and minimal errors
